title: Luther posts the 95 Theses
summary: On 31 October 1517, an obscure Augustinian friar and theology professor named Martin Luther sent a letter to Archbishop Albert of Brandenburg protesting the sale of indulgences in Saxony and enclosing ninety-five Latin theses for academic disputation. Whether he also nailed them to the door of the Castle Church at Wittenberg the same afternoon is disputed, but the text spread within weeks in printed pamphlet form. What began as a scholastic quarrel about penance detonated the Protestant Reformation, split Latin Christendom, and reshaped European politics for centuries.

sections.heading: Background
sections.body:
By 1517 the western Church was the wealthiest institution in Europe and, for many of its own theologians, a scandal. Pope Leo X, a Medici raised in Renaissance Florence, had inherited the vast project of rebuilding St Peter's Basilica in Rome from his predecessor Julius II. To finance it he renewed a plenary indulgence first granted in 1515, promising remission of the temporal punishment of sin for those who confessed and contributed alms. The German end of the operation was managed by Albert of Brandenburg, a young Hohenzollern who already held two bishoprics and had gone deeply into debt to the Fugger bankers of Augsburg to buy the archbishopric of Mainz. Half of the German indulgence revenue was quietly earmarked to repay him.

Albert's chief preacher was the Dominican friar Johann Tetzel, whose theatrical sermons across Saxony and Brandenburg drew large crowds. According to the traditional account, Tetzel's jingle promised that as soon as the coin in the coffer rang, the soul from purgatory sprang. The Elector of Saxony, Frederick the Wise, had banned Tetzel from his own territories, partly because Frederick maintained a rival relic collection at Wittenberg that also carried indulgences. But parishioners from Wittenberg crossed into neighbouring lands to buy the papal letters and returned expecting their confessors to honour them.

One of those confessors was Martin Luther, a thirty-three-year-old friar of the Observant Augustinians and professor of biblical theology at the young University of Wittenberg, founded by Frederick in 1502. Trained in nominalist philosophy and steeped in Augustine and Paul, Luther had spent years wrestling with the problem of how a sinful person could stand before a righteous God. His own reading of the Epistle to the Romans had convinced him that justification came through faith in Christ, not through purchased merit. He was already suspicious of the scholastic apparatus that surrounded penance, and Tetzel's campaign turned his suspicion into public protest.

Luther's response took two forms. He wrote a courteous but sharp letter to Archbishop Albert, warning him that souls were being deceived by his preachers and enclosing a set of theses for academic disputation. In the university tradition of the day such theses were an invitation to argue, not yet a manifesto. He also seems to have sent copies to a few sympathetic scholars, including his colleague Andreas Karlstadt. The letter is dated 31 October 1517, the eve of All Saints, when Wittenberg's church would fill with pilgrims come to view Frederick's relics and gain indulgences of their own.

image.caption: Johann Tetzel, the Dominican indulgence preacher whose Saxony campaign triggered Luther's protest.
image.credit: Portrait attributed to C.G. Bohme, before 1519, via Wikimedia Commons

sections.heading: What happened
sections.body:
Whether Luther actually walked out that afternoon and nailed his ninety-five theses to the north door of All Saints Castle Church at Wittenberg is one of the enduring puzzles of the Reformation. Philip Melanchthon, who was not yet in Wittenberg in 1517, first described the door scene in a preface written after Luther's death in 1546; Luther himself never claimed it in surviving letters. Historians now generally accept that a posting of some kind occurred, perhaps in mid-November, since university custom used the church door as a bulletin board for scholarly disputations. What is certain is that the letter to Albert went out on 31 October, and that Luther considered that date the beginning of his public campaign.

The ninety-five theses themselves are terse Latin propositions, most of them a sentence or two. They open with the assertion that when Christ said 'repent', he meant the whole life of the believer, not a sacramental act. They question whether the pope can remit any penalty except those he has imposed himself, warn that indulgences are dangerous when they make Christians feel secure without true contrition, and end with a series of pointed rhetorical questions: if the pope truly has power to release souls from purgatory, why does he not empty the place at once for love, rather than for money? If Rome is so rich, why does it build St Peter's with the alms of the poor?

The text spread far more quickly than Luther anticipated. Within weeks it was translated from Latin into German, printed as a pamphlet in Nuremberg, Leipzig, and Basel, and passed hand to hand along the trade routes. By early 1518 it was being read in Rome. Curial officials treated Luther first as a nuisance and then as a rebel. Tetzel and the Ingolstadt theologian Konrad Wimpina drafted counter-theses; the Dominican master of the sacred palace, Silvestro Prierias, wrote a hostile Dialogus that framed the dispute as a challenge to papal authority itself.

Luther, summoned to Augsburg in October 1518 to meet the papal legate Cardinal Cajetan, refused to recant. In the Leipzig disputation of 1519 he was pushed by his opponent Johann Eck to concede that both popes and general councils could err, a position that echoed the condemned Bohemian reformer Jan Hus. In June 1520 Leo X issued the bull Exsurge Domine, giving Luther sixty days to submit; on 10 December Luther publicly burnt the bull at Wittenberg's Elster Gate. The following January he was excommunicated. In April 1521 the young emperor Charles V summoned him to the Diet of Worms, where he refused again in the phrase remembered ever after as 'Here I stand.'

image.caption: Ferdinand Pauwels's 1872 painting of Luther posting the 95 Theses on the door of the Wittenberg Castle Church.

sections.heading: Aftermath & consequences
sections.body:
Frederick the Wise, unwilling to hand over his most famous professor, arranged a staged kidnapping on Luther's return journey from Worms and hid him in the Wartburg castle above Eisenach. There, in a few months of frantic work, Luther translated the New Testament into a vigorous, colloquial German that would shape the modern language and put a Reformed Bible into the hands of literate laypeople. By 1525 there were Lutheran city churches from Nuremberg to Riga, and radical reformers such as Andreas Karlstadt and Thomas Müntzer were already pushing further than Luther wished. The German Peasants' War of 1524 to 1525, in which Müntzer took part, was crushed with Luther's own endorsement of princely repression and revealed how quickly religious dissent could become social revolution.

Over the next generation the map of Christendom was redrawn. Huldrych Zwingli reformed Zurich, then died at Kappel in 1531; John Calvin reorganised Geneva after 1541; Henry VIII broke with Rome in 1534 for reasons of dynasty as much as doctrine. The Augsburg Confession of 1530 gave Lutheran territories a formal creed, and the Peace of Augsburg in 1555 recognised the principle that each prince could choose Lutheranism or Catholicism for his own lands. Rome's response, articulated at the Council of Trent from 1545, hardened Catholic doctrine on the very points Luther had raised and launched a disciplined Counter-Reformation staffed by Jesuits, reformed episcopates, and the Roman Inquisition.

The political consequences were as heavy as the theological. Confessional loyalties reshaped alliances, drove waves of refugees across Europe, and produced more than a century of religious warfare that culminated in the Thirty Years' War of 1618 to 1648. The Habsburg dream of a united Catholic empire was permanently blocked; the Dutch broke from Spain in a long revolt that was as much Calvinist as commercial; France endured its own wars of religion, ended only by Henry IV's Edict of Nantes in 1598 and revoked by his grandson in 1685. In Poland-Lithuania, Bohemia, Hungary, and Transylvania, princely families and city magistrates picked among competing Christianities, and the eastern Orthodox world watched from a distance.

image.caption: Martin Luther by Lucas Cranach the Elder, 1529, St Anne's Church, Augsburg.
image.credit: Lucas Cranach the Elder (1529), via Wikimedia Commons

sections.heading: Legacy
sections.body:
Luther himself lived until 1546, a stout and gouty patriarch in Wittenberg with a former nun as his wife, Katharina von Bora, and a stream of table talk faithfully recorded by his students. He never renounced his obedience to Christ or his violent contempt for those who disagreed with him, including in later life the Jewish communities of the German lands, whose treatment he urged in terms that would echo darkly in modern history. He remained a scholar of the biblical text before he was anything else, and his sermons and lectures fill dozens of volumes in the standard Weimar edition.

The long legacy of 31 October 1517 is harder to summarise than the day itself. Protestantism became, over the next five centuries, a family of movements ranging from Lutheran state churches to Anabaptist communities, Pietists, Methodists, Baptists, and, through them, much of the Pentecostal Christianity that grew explosively in the twentieth century across Africa, Asia, and Latin America. The idea that ordinary believers could read the Bible in their own language and judge doctrine against it helped drive vernacular literacy, printing, and, in some readings, the political culture of dissent that later fed movements as different as English republicanism and the American Revolution.

Modern historians resist the older view that Luther single-handedly ushered in the modern world. Late medieval reform movements, the printing press, humanist scholarship, and princely politics all mattered as much as any one theologian. But the ninety-five theses stand as the moment at which a scholastic quarrel about penance became a public confrontation, and every 31 October since 1617 has been marked as Reformation Day by the churches that trace their origin to Wittenberg. Even the Catholic Church, five hundred years later in 2017, joined Lutheran leaders in Lund and Wittenberg to commemorate the anniversary jointly, an ecumenical gesture that would have astonished Luther and Leo X alike.

worldContext.text: Ten months before Luther wrote to Archbishop Albert, the Ottoman sultan Selim I, known to Ottomans as Yavuz, the Grim, had completed the conquest of Mamluk Egypt. His army defeated Sultan Tuman Bay at the battle of Ridaniya outside Cairo on 22 January 1517 and hanged him from the Zuwayla gate; by April the Ottomans had annexed all Mamluk territory from Anatolia to the Hejaz. Selim carried the last Abbasid caliph al-Mutawakkil III and the Prophet's relics back to Constantinople and assumed the title of Khadim al-Haramayn, Servant of the Two Holy Sanctuaries of Mecca and Medina. In one year the Ottoman Empire had roughly doubled in size, absorbed Syria, Egypt, and Arabia, and become the dominant Muslim power in the world; its annual grain shipments from the Nile now fed Istanbul, and its control of the Red Sea drew it into rivalry with Portuguese fleets in the Indian Ocean. In Iran the Safavid shah Ismail I, humiliated at Chaldiran in 1514 by Selim's Janissaries and their artillery, was rebuilding his shattered Qizilbash cavalry and imposing Twelver Shiism as a state religion. In Morocco the Saadian sharifs were consolidating power in the Sous valley.

image.caption: Sultan Selim I of the Ottoman Empire (1470-1520), whose conquests of 1516 to 1517 tripled the empire and gave it Cairo, Damascus, and Jerusalem. Drawing by the German artist Michael Ostendorfer, c. 1548.
image.credit: Michael Ostendorfer (1490 to 1559), via Wikimedia Commons

worldContext.text: Ming China was ruled by the eccentric Zhengde Emperor (Zhu Houzhao), who preferred to live outside the Forbidden City in a personal pleasure residence he called the Leopard Quarter (Baofang), giving himself the pseudonym General Zhu Shou and staging mock military campaigns. On 17 June 1517 a Portuguese squadron of four vessels under Fernao Peres de Andrade sailed into the estuary of the Pearl River, the first official European mission to reach China by sea; Andrade carried the ambassador Tome Pires (author of the great commercial handbook the Suma Oriental) who would eventually be sent to Beijing, though his embassy would collapse when relations with the Portuguese soured after the Battle of Tunmen in 1521 and Pires died in Chinese captivity. Elsewhere the Wang Yangming school of neo-Confucianism, emphasising innate moral knowledge and the unity of knowledge and action, was reshaping Chinese moral philosophy from a Jiangxi provincial post. Coastal piracy, the wokou raiding that mixed Japanese, Chinese, and Portuguese crews, was already becoming the endemic problem it would remain for a century. In Japan, the Muromachi shogunate under Ashikaga Yoshitane presided over the middle Sengoku period, when regional daimyo houses like the Otomo, Mori, and Hojo were consolidating warring domains.

image.caption: Portrait of the Zhengde Emperor (Zhu Houzhao, reigned 1505 to 1521) of the Ming dynasty, at Dingling near Beijing.

worldContext.text: In north India the Delhi Sultanate had passed on 21 November 1517 from the aging Sikandar Lodi, patron of Persian letters and builder of the tomb complex at Bagh-e Alam, to his son Ibrahim Lodi, whose harshness toward his Afghan nobility (including the humiliation of the Lohani and Farmuli clans) would within a decade drive them to invite the Timurid prince Babur down from Kabul into Hindustan; Babur would destroy the sultanate at the First Battle of Panipat on 21 April 1526 with a small army and Ottoman-style field artillery, founding the Mughal Empire. In Persia the young Safavid shah Ismail I, still recovering from his defeat by Selim at Chaldiran in 1514, was consolidating a Shia state that would define the eastern Islamic world for two centuries. In the Deccan the successor sultanates of Bijapur, Golconda, Ahmadnagar, Bidar, and Berar had emerged from the collapse of the Bahmani state, while the Hindu empire of Vijayanagara under Krishnadevaraya (r. 1509 to 1529) was at the peak of its power, its capital at Hampi described by the Portuguese factor Domingo Paes as larger than Rome. Portuguese fortresses at Goa (taken 1510), Hormuz (1515), and Malacca (1511) linked all of these worlds by sea.

image.caption: Babur, the Timurid prince who would found the Mughal Empire in 1526, from the first illustrated Baburnama manuscript, c. 1589 to 1590. Victoria and Albert Museum, London.

worldContext.text: In the West African savannah the Songhai Empire under Askia Muhammad Ture, who had ruled from Gao since 1493 after overthrowing the son of Sunni Ali, stretched from the Atlantic coast of Senegal to the eastern bend of the Niger and controlled the trans-Saharan gold and salt trades that fed the mints of Cairo and Genoa. Under Askia's patronage the University of Sankore at Timbuktu, staffed by scholars like Ahmad Baba's forebears, was among the most active centres of Islamic learning in West Africa; his 1495 hajj had brought back learned Egyptian jurists. In the highlands of Ethiopia the Solomonic emperor Lebna Dengel (r. 1508 to 1540) was in the middle of an uneasy peace with the Muslim sultanate of Adal, a peace that would collapse in the 1520s under the jihadist warlord Ahmad ibn Ibrahim al-Ghazi, known to Ethiopians as Ahmad Gragn, whose armies would nearly destroy the Christian kingdom before Portuguese muskets arrived to save it. On the Swahili coast, Portuguese garrisons at Kilwa and Sofala controlled the outlets of the Zimbabwean gold trade. In the Kongo kingdom, the Christian king Afonso I, baptised at the turn of the century, was writing letters to King Manuel I of Portugal protesting the slave trade already draining his realm.

image.caption: The Sankore Mosque and madrasa at Timbuktu; under Askia Muhammad, Timbuktu was one of the great centres of Islamic scholarship in the Songhai Empire.
image.credit: Dr Ondrej Havelka (HistoriaSalutis), via Wikimedia Commons

worldContext.text: The Aztec Triple Alliance under Moctezuma II Xocoyotzin (r. 1502 to 1520) ruled from Tenochtitlan over most of central Mexico, its markets at Tlatelolco receiving cacao, quetzal feathers, turquoise, cotton, and gold from tributaries as far as the Isthmus of Tehuantepec and the Soconusco. The city, an island metropolis of chinampas and causeways, may have held around 200,000 people, larger than any capital in Europe. In February 1517 a Spanish expedition under Francisco Hernandez de Cordoba sailed from Cuba with 110 men, landed on the Yucatan coast at Cabo Catoche, and was driven off with heavy losses by Maya warriors at Chakan Putun (Champoton); Hernandez himself died of his wounds in Havana. The expedition brought back the first reports of Maya cities to Cuba's governor Diego Velazquez, who dispatched Juan de Grijalva's expedition the following year and Hernan Cortes's the year after. On the Peruvian sierra Huayna Capac, tenth Sapa Inca, was extending Tawantinsuyu northward into what is now Ecuador and southern Colombia from his forward capital at Tumipampa (Tomebamba), unaware that European diseases (smallpox, likely) would reach him from Panama across a chain of coastal contact within a decade and kill him around 1524.

image.caption: Museum model of Tenochtitlan showing the sacred precinct and causeways of the Aztec capital, which under Moctezuma II held perhaps 200,000 residents. National Anthropology Museum, Mexico City.
image.credit: Steve Cadman (from London, UK), via Wikimedia Commons

worldContext.text: No European ship had yet reached the Pacific islands. Ferdinand Magellan's expedition, which would cross the ocean and stumble upon Guam and the Philippines, was still two years from sailing from Sanlucar de Barrameda in August 1519. In New Guinea, Fiji, and Vanuatu, complex trading systems moved obsidian from Talasea, pigs, pottery, and shell valuables across island networks; kula-like exchange circuits linked the Massim archipelagos. Polynesian navigators travelled between Tonga, Samoa, and the Society Islands using star knowledge, wave patterns, and mental sailing directions inherited from long voyages that had already peopled Hawaii around the ninth century and Aotearoa New Zealand around the thirteenth. The Tui Tonga maritime confederation, ruled from Mu'a, extended influence across western Polynesia. In Aotearoa, Maori iwi were consolidating pa hill-fort settlements and refining kumara horticulture; classic Maori material culture (nephrite weapons, waka war canoes, wharenui meeting houses) was reaching its mature form. Australia's Indigenous nations, custodians of some of the oldest continuous cultures on Earth, had lived on the continent for more than sixty thousand years.

image.caption: Uluru at sunset in the central Australian desert, a sacred site of the Anangu people whose ancestors have lived across the continent for over sixty thousand years.

figures.name: Martin Luther
figures.role: Augustinian friar and professor of biblical theology at Wittenberg who wrote the ninety-five theses
figures.credit: Lucas Cranach the Elder (1529), St Anne's Church Augsburg, via Wikimedia Commons

figures.name: Albert of Brandenburg
figures.role: Archbishop of Mainz, whose indulgence campaign to repay the Fuggers provoked Luther's protest

figures.name: Johann Tetzel
figures.role: Dominican indulgence preacher whose sermons in Saxony drew Wittenberg parishioners across the border
figures.credit: Portrait attributed to C.G. Bohme, before 1519, via Wikimedia Commons

figures.name: Pope Leo X
figures.role: Medici pope who authorised the St Peter's indulgence and would excommunicate Luther in 1521
figures.credit: Detail from Raphael's portrait of Leo X, Uffizi Gallery, via Wikimedia Commons

figures.name: Frederick the Wise
figures.role: Elector of Saxony and founder of the University of Wittenberg who protected Luther after Worms

images.caption: Luther Posting the 95 Theses, painted by the Belgian history painter Ferdinand Pauwels in 1872, one of the images that fixed the door-nailing scene in popular memory.
images.credit: Ferdinand Pauwels (1830 to 1904), via Wikimedia Commons

images.caption: Luther at the Diet of Worms in April 1521, refusing to recant before Emperor Charles V. Painted by the German history painter Anton von Werner in 1877 for the Staatsgalerie Stuttgart.
images.credit: Anton von Werner (1843 to 1915), via Wikimedia Commons
